title: Carbon mitigation: 2026 half-time report | Wood Mackenzie
gist: Wood Mackenzie's mid-2026 review finds carbon policy, markets and CCUS had a difficult H1 with pockets of resilience: EU carbon demand dipped then recovered, one regional carbon market surged 76%, buyer-concentration risk in offsets was exposed by Microsoft, and CCUS pipelines grew even as little new capacity came online.

insights.claim: The Middle East conflict and member-state opposition to the EU Emissions Trading Scheme softened EU carbon market demand in Q1 2026, but demand recovered in Q2.
insights.speaker: Stephen Vogado
insights.so_what: EU carbon demand proved cyclically resilient after a geopolitics-driven Q1 dip; a buy-the-dip signal for compliance-market exposure.
insights.locator: The Middle East conflict and member-state opposition

insights.claim: Alberta introduced less ambitious pricing for its TIER regulations and California's WCI prices remain subdued with proposed amendments likely to soften demand further, showing North American carbon markets weakening.
insights.speaker: Stephen Vogado
insights.so_what: Policy dilution in Alberta and California is bearish for North American carbon-allowance prices.
insights.locator: In Canada, Alberta introduced less ambitious pricing

insights.claim: Prices for one regional carbon market have already surged 76% in 2026, a standout outperformer amid otherwise soft carbon demand.
insights.speaker: Stephen Vogado
insights.so_what: A 76% rally signals dispersion across regional carbon markets; identifying the outperformer is the actionable edge.
insights.quote: prices for one regional carbon market have already surged 76% in 2026
insights.locator: More positively, prices for one regional carbon market

insights.claim: Microsoft's announcement that it may adjust the pace or volume of its carbon removal procurement exposed the offset market's buyer concentration risk.
insights.speaker: Stephen Vogado
insights.so_what: Carbon-removal demand is dangerously reliant on one buyer; Microsoft's pace is a leading indicator for the whole market.
insights.quote: Microsoft's announcement it may 'adjust the pace or volume' of its carbon removal procurement exposed the market's buyer concentration risk

insights.claim: Recent EU proposals and expected H2 2026 announcements should reinvigorate the European offset market's stalled growth trajectory.
insights.speaker: Stephen Vogado
insights.so_what: EU policy is the potential catalyst to unstick European offset demand; watch H2 announcements.
insights.locator: On the plus side, recent EU proposals

insights.claim: In H1 2026 the overall CCUS project pipeline grew strongly but only a fraction of expected new capacity became operational, with headline projects facing supply chain issues, permitting hurdles and construction delays.
insights.speaker: Stephen Vogado
insights.so_what: The gap between pipeline and operational capacity is the key execution risk for CCUS; delivery, not announcements, is what matters.
insights.locator: For carbon capture, utilisation and storage (CCUS), H1 2026 saw

insights.claim: CCUS in H1 2026 was a mixed and highly regionalised picture, with several countries making positive policy announcements offsetting delays and setbacks elsewhere.
insights.speaker: Stephen Vogado
insights.so_what: CCUS opportunity is geography-specific; assess by jurisdiction rather than as a single global market.
insights.locator: CCUS: A mixed and highly regionalised picture

themes.theme: Carbon policy: phaseout, litigation, Global South leadership

insights.claim: The First Conference on Transitioning Away from Fossil Fuels shifted the narrative, with 50 countries including the EU, UK, Canada, Brazil, Nigeria and joint hosts Colombia and The Netherlands placing fossil fuel phaseout front and centre.
insights.speaker: Stephen Vogado
insights.so_what: Multilateral phaseout momentum keeps long-term policy pressure on fossil fuel assets despite near-term setbacks.
insights.locator: The First Conference on Transitioning Away from Fossil Fuels

insights.claim: The UN General Assembly gave strong backing for climate litigation, overwhelmingly adopting a resolution supporting the International Court of Justice's ruling on countries' obligation to protect the environment from greenhouse gas emissions.
insights.speaker: Stephen Vogado
insights.so_what: Institutionalised climate litigation raises legal/liability risk for high-emitting sectors and states.
insights.locator: The UN General Assembly gave strong backing

insights.claim: Colombia emerged as an important moral and diplomatic voice on climate pushing beyond incremental multilateralism, while Brazil demonstrated continuing momentum, even as the failure of other countries to show clear climate leadership was a negative.
insights.speaker: Stephen Vogado
insights.so_what: Leadership is shifting toward Global South actors; a leadership vacuum elsewhere tempers overall policy momentum.
insights.locator: Colombia emerged as an important moral
